metadata.purpose: Study material knowledge base for quiz, flashcard, and tutor generation

legal_studies.description: Exposes students to 'law in context,' helping them understand how law operates in society while developing practical skills to analyze legal phenomena and investigate legal resources.

courses.name: The Legal Environment of Business
courses.description: Emphasizes the nature of legal systems and processes. Topics include agency, contracts, the Uniform Commercial Code, debtor-creditor relationships, government regulation of business, and business structure selection.

courses.name: Introduction to the U.S. Legal System
courses.description: Provides foundational overview of U.S. legal system including sources of law, court system, constitutional law, civil law and procedure, criminal law and procedure, and the regulatory state. Explores connections to international legal systems.

courses.name: Global Legal Traditions
courses.description: Introduces comparative law, examining how laws vary across nations within major legal traditions including common law. Analyzes institutions, substantive law, founding concepts, attitudes toward change, and relationships among traditions.

courses.name: Criminal Law and Procedure
courses.description: Foundations of criminal responsibility, common crime definitions, and procedural requirements. Topics include guilt establishment, justification of punishment, criminal conduct definition, inchoate crimes, right to counsel, search warrants, jury selection, negotiated pleas, and evidence rules.

courses.name: Civil Rights and Liberties
courses.description: Examines legal principles and rules defining the nature and limits of American government and constitutional citizen protections. Emphasizes Supreme Court decision analysis and influence on American political and economic development.

courses.name: Communications Law
courses.description: Studies legal rights and privileges of communications media addressing written communications, privacy vs. transparency, libel, defamation, copyright, infringement, regulatory agencies, and First Amendment theories.

courses.name: Law and the Digital World
courses.description: Covers legal and policy issues regarding technology's societal impact. Topics: internet infrastructure regulation, individual rights in cyberspace, protection from cyber threats, and private company regulation.

courses.name: Law and Society
courses.description: Examines law's nature and societal limitations, legal categories shaping society, roles of lawyers and judges, judiciary structure, case flow, and controversial issues in business, medicine, and gender. Draws from sociology, political science, history, and philosophy.

courses.name: Sport and the Law
courses.description: Uses sport as an analytical lens for examining societal changes and interactions across local, regional, national, and international levels.

courses.name: International Law
courses.description: Introduces binding rules, norms, and principles governing state interactions. Covers sources, sovereignty, jurisdiction, state responsibility, treaties, non-intervention, dispute resolution, international organizations, criminal law, force use, terrorism, and environmental law.

courses.name: Nonprofit Law and Governance
courses.description: Explores law, governance, and policy surrounding the nonprofit sector. Addresses formation, maintenance, and governance of tax-exempt organizations, director/officer duties and liabilities, and alternatives like fiscal sponsorship.

courses.name: Health Law
courses.description: How law regulates health and affects practitioners, patients, and scientists. Topics vary but include infectious disease, privacy, quarantine, FDA regulation, clinical trials, advertisement, medical tourism, reproductive health, rationing, abortion, and end-of-life issues.

courses.name: History, Law, and the Holocaust
courses.description: Explores the Holocaust and post-1945 international law development. Covers anti-Semitism, Hitler's rise, the Final Solution, Nuremberg Tribunal, peremptory norms, transitional justice, hate speech, and Holocaust denial.

courses.name: Markets and the Law: The Uniform Commercial Code
courses.description: Advanced examination of UCC provisions covering contracts, sales, negotiable instruments, and secured transactions. Particularly relevant for accounting-interested students.

courses.name: Law of Financial Institutions
courses.description: Laws and regulations governing U.S. financial institutions and federal regulatory agencies. Analyzes monetary system and capital markets evolution. Examines the 2007-2008 financial crisis and regulatory responses, focusing on Dodd-Frank Act.

courses.name: Gender and the Law
courses.description: Gender-related legal history, social battles, and legal analysis. Topics include voting rights, privacy, affirmative action, abortion, reproductive rights, rape laws, domestic violence, human trafficking, and discrimination based on sex and sexual orientation.

courses.name: Legal Ethics
courses.description: Investigates ethical systems guiding thought about 'the Good' as the foundation of law's purpose. Integrates ethical systems with moral development theories using U.S. and international law examples.

courses.name: International Business Law
courses.description: Government control of international business and overseas business processes. Compares U.S., European, Japanese, and Middle Eastern cultures and legal systems. Focuses on international agreements including GATT, NAFTA, and EU.

courses.name: Seminar in Politics and Law
courses.prerequisites: Politics and Law major; Senior standing
courses.description: Interdisciplinary capstone with selected theme for in-depth examination. Students complete major research projects presented to seminar. Cross-listed with POLS 490.

courses.name: Directed Study in Legal Studies
courses.description: Faculty-supervised study of well-defined legal studies interest areas.

international_business.degree: Bachelor of Science in International Business
international_business.description: Develops strong theoretical and practical foundation in international business through globally focused coursework in business and liberal arts. Requires foreign language minor and global learning experiences.
learning_objectives: Lead responsibly in global business contexts
Understand cultural, institutional, and governmental factors affecting international operations
Demonstrate functional business expertise with concentration focus
Apply problem-solving skills to complex global business challenges
Communicate effectively in English and a foreign language
Leverage study abroad, internships, and consulting projects

courses.name: International Business Management
courses.description: Overview of the cultural, economic, legal, and political forces that shape the environment of international business.

courses.name: Special Topics in International Business
courses.description: Topics vary yearly based on student interest, faculty availability, and current developments in IB or functional areas.

courses.name: Financial and Economic Developments in Latin America
courses.description: Survey of contemporary financial and economic environment in Latin America with focus on doing business in Mexico, Chile, Brazil, and Argentina. Examines social, economic, and political forces affecting business.

courses.name: Internship in International Business
courses.prerequisites: BSIB major; 2.5+ GPA; Faculty approval; Junior/Senior standing
courses.description: Supervised employment applying IB theory. Minimum 10 hours weekly with faculty meetings, research, and substantive report.

courses.name: International Business Practicum
courses.description: Capstone combining global business strategy and practical experience. Students serve as consultants analyzing market entry and competitive strategies.

courses.name: Directed Study in International Business
courses.prerequisites: BSIB major; 3.0+ GPA; Faculty approval; Senior standing
courses.description: In-depth study or research under faculty direction in internationally focused areas.

entrepreneurship.description: Dedicated entrepreneurship degree combining core entrepreneurship courses with a specialized concentration and business foundation coursework. 120 credit hours minimum.
core_requirements: ENT 280 - Creating a New Venture (3 cr)

courses.name: Creating a New Venture
courses.description: Searching the environment for new venture opportunities, matching individual skills with the venture, evaluating viability, writing a business plan, and financing/starting the new venture.

courses.name: Entrepreneurial Marketing
courses.prerequisites: MKT 201, MKT 201G, or MKT 203; Junior standing
courses.description: Examines concepts and strategic issues for startup ventures, including customer base creation and marketing under financial constraints.

courses.name: Entrepreneurial Finance
courses.description: Financing and growing a new venture whether inside or outside the corporate structure. Applies financial tools and analytic techniques to new venture creation and growth with a global perspective.

courses.name: Entrepreneurship in Action
courses.description: Students team with entrepreneurs in an incubator-like environment, gaining hands-on experience in sales, product development, and fundraising.

courses.name: Managing a New Venture
courses.description: Develops general management and entrepreneurial leadership skills through case studies across diverse industries.

courses.name: Entrepreneurship Practicum
courses.description: Students in small teams assigned to a local startup conducting due diligence, developing business plans, and preparing pitch decks.

courses.name: Directed Study in Entrepreneurship
courses.description: In-depth study or research under faculty direction for seniors concentrating in Entrepreneurship.

psychology.college: School of Health and Behavioral Sciences
psychology.degree: Bachelor of Science with a Major in Psychology
psychology.description: Emphasizes theoretical and applied psychology plus exposure to core business courses. Minimum 36 credits for major, 120 for degree. Requires a business minor.
core_requirements: PSY 260 or 263 - Introduction to Psychology (3 cr)
PSY 376 - Research Methods in Psychology (3 cr)
PSY 490 or 491 - Senior Research/Internship Seminar (3 cr)

courses.name: Introduction to Psychology
courses.description: Survey of major principles, theories, and research methods in psychology, covering mental processing, behavior across contexts, and ethical implications.

courses.name: Sensation and Perception
courses.description: Explores biological and psychological processes of perception, sensory systems, brain processing structures, and integration of sensory information.

courses.name: Psychology of Personality
courses.description: Historical and contemporary personality approaches, development, cross-cultural and gender influences, with application to case studies.

courses.name: Introduction to Psychopathology
courses.description: Contemporary approaches to understanding causes and treatments of psychological and psychiatric disorders.

courses.name: Child and Adolescent Development
courses.description: Development from prenatal through adolescence using current research methods and relevant theories.

courses.name: Adult Development and Aging
courses.description: Psychological and physical change and stability throughout adulthood, emphasizing aging individuals in an aging society.

courses.name: Environmental Psychology
courses.description: Interdisciplinary investigation of the environment's role in behavior and attributes of settings associated with performance and functioning.

courses.name: Applied Psychology
courses.description: Overview of practical applications of psychological research to real-world problems in biomedical, educational, industrial/organizational, sports, and other contexts.

courses.name: Positive Psychology
courses.description: Subjective well-being, optimal human functioning, positive traits development, and collective societal well-being across the lifespan.

courses.name: Cognitive Psychology
courses.description: Primary cognitive psychology areas including neuroscience, perception, attention, memory, language, and decision-making.

courses.name: Introduction to Neuroscience
courses.description: Foundational overview of nervous system structure and function including neurons, neural communication, brain organization, sensory systems, and neural basis of behavior.

courses.name: Health Psychology
courses.description: Stress, coping, lifestyle enhancement, chronic illness, pain management, and utilization of healthcare systems.

courses.name: Research Methods in Psychology
courses.description: Introduction to experimental methods covering research planning, design, statistics, APA report writing, ethical standards, and critical evaluation of scientific evidence.

courses.name: Educational Psychology
courses.description: Psychological principles and theories applied to teaching and learning, covering motivation, developmental characteristics, assessment, and socio-cultural influences.

courses.name: Industrial and Organizational Psychology
courses.description: Workplace human behavior, employee hiring, management, development, and career support aligned with organizational strategic goals.

courses.name: Neural Circuits and Systems
courses.description: How neural circuits produce sensation, action, attention, executive function, language, emotion, motivation, and sleep, including disruption effects.

courses.name: Research Methods in Psychology II
courses.prerequisites: PSY 260 or 263, MATH 201, PSY 376; Sophomore standing
courses.description: Advanced research methods focusing on design, implementation, and analysis of psychological research for graduate preparation.

courses.name: Neurobiology of Learning and Memory
courses.description: Current understanding of learning and memory processes from human and animal research, comparing memory types and underlying mechanisms.

courses.name: The Design Thinking Process
courses.description: Hands-on application of design thinking and psychological principles underlying innovative thinking, problem-solving, and gamification.

courses.name: Cross-Cultural Psychology
courses.description: In-depth examination of culture's role in socialization and behavior using cross-cultural methodology.

courses.name: Social Psychology
courses.description: Factors impacting human relationships including attraction, attitudes, perception, cognition, altruism, aggression, group behavior, and social influence.

courses.name: Gender in Childhood
courses.description: Gender meaning and its influence on children's experiences, identities, and behavior considering biology and socio-cultural factors.

courses.name: Child Psychopathology
courses.description: Atypical childhood and adolescent development including behavioral, mood, and learning disorders within normal development and systems contexts.

courses.name: Counseling Theory and Practice
courses.description: Major contemporary counseling theories and techniques with observation opportunities and practice, addressing cross-cultural issues.

courses.name: Exercise and Sport Psychology
courses.description: Educational, research, and professional contributions of psychology to promote, enhance, and maintain exercise and sport behavior across the lifespan.

courses.name: Forensic Psychology
courses.description: Criminal profiling, hypnosis, lie detection, eyewitness testimony, jury selection, insanity defense, and related topics.

courses.name: Drugs and Behavior
courses.description: Drugs and behavioral effects covering stimulants, depressants, narcotics, hallucinogens, and psychiatric medications with historical perspectives.

courses.name: Psychological Testing and Assessment
courses.description: Assessment goals and principles including measurement theory, test construction, major test types, and contemporary issues like bias and ethics.

courses.name: Judgment and Decision Making
courses.description: Research on judgment and decision-making with exploration of influence in health, financial, legal, political, and relationship choice contexts.

courses.name: Senior Research Seminar
courses.prerequisites: PSY 260 or 263, PSY 371, PSY 376; Applied Psychology major; Senior standing
courses.description: Students develop and investigate applied psychology hypotheses from hypothesis through presentations, integrating three years of knowledge.

courses.name: Senior Internship Seminar
courses.prerequisites: PSY 260 or 263, PSY 376; Psychology major; Senior standing
courses.description: Integration course with field placement and classroom seminar involving lectures, exercises, discussions, and written assignments.

sources_of_law.definition: The origins from which legal authority and rules derive in a legal system.

constitutional_law.definition: The supreme law of a nation or state, establishing governmental structure, powers, and individual rights.

landmark_cases.significance: Established judicial review, giving courts power to declare laws unconstitutional.
Affirmed implied powers of Congress under the Necessary and Proper Clause; established federal supremacy over states.
Broadly interpreted the Commerce Clause, giving Congress power over interstate commerce.
Declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional under the Equal Protection Clause.

examples: The First Amendment protects freedom of speech from government restriction.
The Commerce Clause allows federal regulation of activities substantially affecting interstate commerce.

statutory_law.definition: Written laws enacted by legislative bodies (Congress, state legislatures, local councils).
examples: The Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990

administrative_law.definition: Rules, regulations, and orders created by administrative agencies (executive branch) that have the force of law.

landmark_cases.significance: Established two-step test for judicial deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous statutes.

common_law.definition: Judge-made law developed through court decisions and precedent (stare decisis), rather than statutes.
examples: Much of contract law and tort law evolved through common law
The 'reasonable person' standard in negligence emerged from judicial decisions

sources_of_law.relationships: Constitutional law is supreme; statutes must conform to the Constitution. Administrative regulations must be authorized by enabling statutes. Common law fills gaps where statutes are silent but can be superseded by statutory law.

contract_law.definition: The body of law governing legally enforceable agreements between two or more parties.

offer.definition: A manifestation of willingness to enter into a bargain, made so that another person is justified in understanding that assent will conclude the bargain.
rules: Must be communicated to the offeree
Must have definite and certain terms
Terminated by: revocation, rejection, counteroffer, lapse of time, death/incapacity, illegality

acceptance.definition: A voluntary act by the offeree that shows assent to the terms of the offer.
rules: Must be by the person to whom the offer was made
Must match the offer (mirror image rule at common law; UCC is more flexible)
Must be communicated to the offeror

consideration.definition: Something of legal value exchanged between parties; a bargained-for exchange of promises or performances.

landmark_cases.significance: Established that forbearance (refraining from a legal right) constitutes valid consideration.
A gratuitous promise (gift) is not enforceable for lack of consideration.

capacity.definition: The legal ability to enter into a contract. Minors, mentally incapacitated persons, and intoxicated persons may lack capacity.

legality.definition: The contract's purpose must be legal. Contracts for illegal purposes are void and unenforceable.

breach.definition: Failure to perform a contractual duty without legal excuse.

types.material_breach: A substantial failure that permits the non-breaching party to treat the contract as terminated and sue for damages.
types.minor_breach: A slight deviation; the non-breaching party must still perform but may sue for damages caused by the breach.
types.anticipatory_breach: One party indicates in advance they will not perform; the other party may immediately sue or wait for performance.

landmark_cases.significance: Established the foreseeability test for consequential damages in breach of contract.
Established the substantial performance doctrine: a party who substantially performs is entitled to payment minus damages for deviation.

remedies.compensatory_damages: Money to restore the injured party to the position they would have been in had the contract been performed (expectation interest).
remedies.consequential_damages: Foreseeable indirect losses resulting from the breach (established in Hadley v. Baxendale).
remedies.liquidated_damages: Pre-agreed damages specified in the contract; enforceable if reasonable estimate of actual damages.
remedies.specific_performance: Court order requiring the breaching party to perform; typically for unique goods or real property.
remedies.rescission_and_restitution: Canceling the contract and restoring both parties to pre-contract positions.
remedies.nominal_damages: Small symbolic amount when breach occurred but no actual loss.

tort_law.definition: The body of law addressing civil wrongs that cause harm to persons or property, providing remedies (usually damages) to injured parties.

negligence.definition: Failure to exercise the degree of care that a reasonably prudent person would exercise under similar circumstances.
elements: Duty of care (owed to foreseeable plaintiffs)
Breach of duty (failure to meet standard of care)
Causation: actual cause (but-for test) AND proximate cause (foreseeability)

landmark_cases.significance: Established the foreseeability test for proximate cause: duty is owed only to foreseeable plaintiffs.
Eliminated the privity requirement for negligence claims involving dangerous products.
Hot coffee case illustrating product liability, foreseeability, and comparative fault analysis.

strict_liability.definition: Liability without fault; defendant is liable regardless of intent or negligence.
applies_to: Product liability (manufacturing defects, design defects, failure to warn)

landmark_cases.significance: Established strict liability for non-natural use of land causing damage.
Established strict liability in tort for defective products, eliminating need to prove negligence.

intentional_torts.definition: Wrongful acts committed with intent to cause harm or with substantial certainty that harm will result.

against_persons: False imprisonment (intentional confinement without consent or authority)
against_property: Conversion (wrongful exercise of dominion over another's property)

business_organizations.definition: Legal structures through which business activities are conducted.

sole_proprietorship.definition: An unincorporated business owned and operated by one individual.

general_partnership.definition: An association of two or more persons carrying on a business as co-owners for profit (UPA).
characteristics: May be formed by oral agreement
Each partner is an agent of the partnership

limited_partnership.definition: Partnership with at least one general partner (unlimited liability, management) and one or more limited partners (liability limited to investment, no management role).

limited_liability_partnership.definition: Partnership where all partners have limited liability for the acts of other partners.

corporation.definition: A legal entity separate from its owners (shareholders), created under state law.

types.c_corporation: Standard corporation subject to double taxation (corporate level + shareholder dividend level).
types.s_corporation: Elects pass-through taxation; limited to 100 shareholders, one class of stock, U.S. shareholders only.
types.close_corporation: Small number of shareholders; stock not publicly traded; may operate without a formal board.

landmark_cases.significance: Established that a corporate charter is a contract protected by the Constitution.
Recognized corporate First Amendment rights regarding political speech.

limited_liability_company.definition: A hybrid entity combining limited liability of a corporation with pass-through taxation and operational flexibility of a partnership.

employment_law.definition: The body of law governing the employer-employee relationship.

at_will_employment.definition: Either employer or employee can terminate the relationship at any time, for any reason not prohibited by law.

statutes: Title VII of the Civil Rights Act (1964) - race, color, religion, sex, national origin
Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA) - age 40+

workers_compensation.definition: No-fault insurance system providing benefits to employees injured on the job, in exchange for employees giving up the right to sue employers for negligence.

landmark_cases.significance: Established the disparate impact theory under Title VII.
Recognized hostile work environment sexual harassment as actionable under Title VII.

intellectual_property.definition: Legal rights protecting creations of the mind, granting exclusive rights to creators/inventors.

patents.definition: Exclusive rights granted for an invention, allowing the holder to exclude others from making, using, or selling the invention for a limited period.
patents.duration: 20 years from filing date (utility); 15 years from grant date (design)
types: Utility patent (new/useful process, machine, manufacture, composition)

trademarks.definition: A word, phrase, symbol, design, or combination that identifies and distinguishes the source of goods or services.
trademarks.duration: Potentially indefinite (must renew every 10 years; must remain in use)

landmark_cases.case: Qualitex Co. v. Jacobson Products Co. (1995)
landmark_cases.significance: Color alone can serve as a trademark if it has acquired secondary meaning.

copyrights.definition: Protection for original works of authorship fixed in a tangible medium of expression.
copyrights.duration: Life of author + 70 years (individual); 95 years from publication or 120 years from creation (work for hire)
fair_use_factors: Purpose and character of the use (commercial vs. educational; transformative?)
Amount and substantiality of the portion used
Effect on the market for the original work

trade_secrets.definition: Information that derives economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
trade_secrets.duration: Potentially indefinite (as long as secrecy is maintained)

ethics_in_business_and_law.definition: The application of ethical principles and moral standards to business activities and legal practice.

ethical_theories.utilitarianism: Actions are right if they produce the greatest good for the greatest number (Bentham, Mill).
ethical_theories.deontological_ethics: Actions are right or wrong based on adherence to rules or duties, regardless of consequences (Kant's categorical imperative).
ethical_theories.virtue_ethics: Focuses on the character of the moral agent rather than rules or consequences (Aristotle).
ethical_theories.social_contract_theory: Moral/political rules are justified by agreement among individuals (Rawls' veil of ignorance).
ethical_theories.ethics_of_care: Emphasizes relationships and responsibilities rather than abstract principles.

business_ethics_frameworks.stakeholder_theory: Businesses should consider the interests of all stakeholders (employees, customers, suppliers, community, shareholders).
business_ethics_frameworks.shareholder_primacy: The primary duty of a corporation is to maximize shareholder value (Friedman doctrine).
business_ethics_frameworks.corporate_social_responsibility: Businesses have obligations beyond profit-making, including social and environmental responsibilities.
business_ethics_frameworks.triple_bottom_line: People, Planet, Profit - measuring success across social, environmental, and financial dimensions.

key_legislation: Sarbanes-Oxley Act (2002) - corporate governance and financial disclosure
Dodd-Frank Act (2010) - financial reform and consumer protection

ucc_commercial_transactions.definition: The Uniform Commercial Code is a comprehensive set of laws governing commercial transactions in the United States, adopted (with variations) by all 50 states.

key_articles.article_1: General Provisions (definitions, rules of construction, good faith obligation)

article_2.name: Sales of Goods
key_concepts: Applies to transactions in goods (movable, tangible property)
Implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose
differences_from_common_law: More flexible acceptance rules (no strict mirror image)
Firm offer rule (merchant's signed written offer irrevocable for stated period up to 3 months)

agency_law.definition: The body of law governing the relationship where one party (the agent) is authorized to act on behalf of another (the principal).
formation: Apparent agency (third party reasonably believes agent has authority)

types_of_authority.actual_express: Authority explicitly granted by the principal.
types_of_authority.actual_implied: Authority reasonably necessary to carry out express authority.
types_of_authority.apparent: Authority that a reasonable third party would believe the agent has based on the principal's conduct.

liability.disclosed_principal: Principal is liable; agent generally not liable on contracts.
liability.partially_disclosed_principal: Both principal and agent may be liable.
liability.undisclosed_principal: Agent is liable; principal may also be liable once discovered.
liability.vicarious_liability: Principal liable for agent's torts committed within scope of employment (respondeat superior).

landmark_cases.significance: Established that an agency relationship can be implied from conduct, even without an express agreement.

globalization_theories.definition: Globalization is the increasing interconnectedness and interdependence of world economies, cultures, and populations through cross-border trade, technology, investment, and migration.

perspectives.hyperglobalist: National borders are becoming irrelevant; a single global market is emerging (Ohmae).
perspectives.skeptic: Globalization is overstated; nation-states and regionalism remain dominant (Hirst & Thompson).
perspectives.transformationalist: Globalization is real but its direction is uncertain; states are being transformed, not diminished (Held, Giddens).

drivers: Political factors (end of Cold War, free-market ideology)
criticisms: Race to the bottom in labor/environmental standards

mercantilism.definition: A nation's wealth depends on accumulated gold/silver; maximize exports, minimize imports (trade surplus).
mercantilism.criticism: Zero-sum thinking; ignores mutual gains from trade.

absolute_advantage.definition: A country should produce and export goods it can produce more efficiently than other countries (Adam Smith, 1776).
absolute_advantage.example: If the U.S. can produce wheat more efficiently than Japan, and Japan can produce electronics more efficiently than the U.S., each should specialize and trade.

comparative_advantage.definition: A country should specialize in producing goods for which it has the lowest opportunity cost, even if it doesn't have absolute advantage in anything (David Ricardo, 1817).
comparative_advantage.example: Even if the U.S. is more efficient at producing both wheat and cloth than Bangladesh, if Bangladesh's opportunity cost of cloth is lower, Bangladesh should specialize in cloth.

heckscher_ohlin.definition: Countries export goods that intensively use their abundant factors of production and import goods that intensively use their scarce factors (Heckscher & Ohlin).
heckscher_ohlin.example: Capital-abundant countries (U.S., Germany) export capital-intensive goods; labor-abundant countries (Bangladesh, Vietnam) export labor-intensive goods.

new_trade_theory.definition: Economies of scale and first-mover advantages can give countries an edge; trade may arise even between similar countries (Paul Krugman).

porters_diamond.definition: Michael Porter's model explaining why certain industries in certain nations are more competitive, based on four attributes.
factors: Related and supporting industries (presence of supplier clusters)
Firm strategy, structure, and rivalry (domestic competition)

foreign_exchange_markets.definition: The global marketplace for exchanging national currencies, the largest and most liquid financial market in the world.

key_concepts.exchange_rate: The price of one currency expressed in terms of another.
key_concepts.spot_rate: The exchange rate for immediate delivery (within 2 business days).
key_concepts.forward_rate: The exchange rate agreed upon today for delivery at a future date.
key_concepts.appreciation: An increase in the value of a currency relative to another.
key_concepts.depreciation: A decrease in the value of a currency relative to another.
key_concepts.bid_ask_spread: The difference between the buying and selling price of a currency.

exchange_rate_systems.fixed: Government/central bank pegs currency to another currency or basket (e.g., gold standard, currency board).
exchange_rate_systems.floating: Exchange rate determined by supply and demand in the forex market (e.g., USD, EUR, JPY).
exchange_rate_systems.managed_float: Primarily market-determined but with occasional central bank intervention (dirty float).
exchange_rate_systems.pegged: Currency value fixed relative to a major currency (e.g., Hong Kong dollar pegged to USD).

determinants: Interest rates (higher rates attract capital, appreciate currency)
Inflation rates (higher inflation depreciates currency via PPP)
Trade balance (surplus tends to appreciate currency)

types: Transaction exposure (risk on outstanding FX-denominated obligations)
Translation exposure (risk from consolidating foreign subsidiary financials)
Economic exposure (long-term impact of exchange rate changes on firm value)
hedging_techniques: Natural hedging (matching revenues/costs in same currency)

key_topics.international_capital_budgeting: Evaluating foreign investment projects considering exchange rate risk, political risk, tax differences, and blocked funds.
key_topics.cost_of_capital: Firms access global capital markets to lower cost of capital through international diversification.

cross_cultural_management.definition: Understanding and managing the impact of cultural differences on business practices and organizational behavior.

power_distance.definition: The extent to which less powerful members of organizations accept that power is distributed unequally.
power_distance.high_pd: Malaysia, Philippines, Mexico - hierarchical, centralized authority, deference to superiors
power_distance.low_pd: Denmark, Austria, Israel - flat organizations, participative management, questioning authority

individualism_vs_collectivism.definition: The degree to which people are integrated into groups versus acting as individuals.
individualism_vs_collectivism.individualist: U.S., UK, Australia - personal achievement, individual rights, loose social ties
individualism_vs_collectivism.collectivist: Japan, China, Colombia - group harmony, loyalty, in-group obligations

masculinity_vs_femininity.definition: Distribution of emotional roles between genders; assertiveness/competitiveness vs. modesty/caring.
masculinity_vs_femininity.masculine: Japan, Hungary, Austria - competition, achievement, material success
masculinity_vs_femininity.feminine: Sweden, Norway, Netherlands - quality of life, consensus, gender equality

uncertainty_avoidance.definition: Society's tolerance for ambiguity and uncertainty.
uncertainty_avoidance.high_ua: Greece, Portugal, Japan - formal rules, risk aversion, expert authority
uncertainty_avoidance.low_ua: Singapore, Denmark, UK - tolerance of deviance, risk-taking, fewer rules

long_term_vs_short_term_orientation.definition: The extent to which a society prioritizes future-oriented values (thrift, perseverance) vs. past/present-oriented values (tradition, social obligations).

indulgence_vs_restraint.definition: The extent to which a society allows free gratification of basic human desires vs. regulating through strict norms.

other_frameworks.trompenaars: Seven dimensions of culture (universalism/particularism, individualism/communitarianism, specific/diffuse, neutral/emotional, achievement/ascription, sequential/synchronic, internal/external control).
other_frameworks.hall: High-context vs. low-context cultures; monochronic vs. polychronic time.
other_frameworks.globe_study: Extension of Hofstede covering 62 societies with 9 cultural dimensions.

modes_of_entry.definition: The methods by which a firm enters a foreign market, ranging from low-risk/low-control to high-risk/high-control.

exporting.definition: Producing goods domestically and selling them abroad.
types: Direct exporting (selling directly to foreign customers)
Indirect exporting (using intermediaries like export trading companies)

licensing.definition: Granting a foreign entity the right to use intellectual property (patents, trademarks, technology) in exchange for royalties.

franchising.definition: A specialized form of licensing where the franchisor provides a complete business system to the franchisee.

joint_venture.definition: Two or more firms create a jointly owned entity to operate in a foreign market.
advantages: May be required by host government

wholly_owned_subsidiary.definition: The firm owns 100% of a foreign operation, either through greenfield investment (building new) or acquisition.

foreign_direct_investment.definition: Investment in physical assets or significant ownership stake in a foreign business entity.

wto.name: World Trade Organization
wto.predecessor: GATT (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade, 1947)
wto.function: Administers trade agreements, provides forum for negotiations, handles trade disputes.
key_principles: Most-Favored Nation (MFN) - treat all members equally
National Treatment - treat foreign and domestic goods equally once imported
wto.dispute_settlement: Binding dispute resolution mechanism; panels and Appellate Body hear cases.

imf.name: International Monetary Fund
imf.function: Promotes global monetary cooperation, financial stability, facilitates trade, reduces poverty.

world_bank.name: World Bank Group
world_bank.function: Provides loans and grants to developing countries for capital programs aimed at reducing poverty.

regional_agreements.eu: European Union - most advanced economic integration (customs union, single market, monetary union for eurozone).
regional_agreements.usmca: United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (replaced NAFTA in 2020) - free trade area.
regional_agreements.asean: Association of Southeast Asian Nations - economic cooperation among 10 Southeast Asian countries.
regional_agreements.mercosur: Southern Common Market - customs union of South American countries.
regional_agreements.cptpp: Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership.

global_supply_chain_management.definition: The management of the flow of goods, services, information, and finances across international borders from raw materials to end consumers.

key_concepts.logistics: Physical movement and storage of goods (transportation, warehousing, inventory management).
key_concepts.procurement: Sourcing and acquiring inputs from global suppliers.
key_concepts.distribution: Getting finished products to customers in multiple markets.

strategies.offshoring: Moving business operations to a foreign country (typically for cost savings).
strategies.nearshoring: Moving operations to a nearby country (balance cost and proximity).
strategies.reshoring: Bringing operations back to the home country.
strategies.outsourcing: Contracting with external firms (domestic or foreign) to perform functions.

business_model_canvas.definition: A strategic management tool (Alexander Osterwalder) that provides a visual chart with nine building blocks describing a firm's value proposition, infrastructure, customers, and finances.

nine_blocks.customer_segments: Who are the target customers? (Mass market, niche, segmented, multi-sided platform)
nine_blocks.value_propositions: What value is delivered to the customer? What problem is being solved? (Newness, performance, customization, design, brand, price, cost reduction, risk reduction, accessibility, convenience)
nine_blocks.channels: How does the company reach and communicate with its customer segments? (Direct, indirect, owned, partner channels)
nine_blocks.customer_relationships: What type of relationship does each customer segment expect? (Personal assistance, self-service, automated, communities, co-creation)
nine_blocks.revenue_streams: How does the company generate revenue from each segment? (Asset sale, usage fee, subscription, licensing, advertising, freemium)
nine_blocks.key_resources: What assets are required? (Physical, intellectual, human, financial)
nine_blocks.key_activities: What key things must the company do? (Production, problem solving, platform/network management)
nine_blocks.key_partnerships: Who are key partners and suppliers? (Strategic alliances, coopetition, joint ventures, buyer-supplier)
nine_blocks.cost_structure: What are the most important costs? (Cost-driven vs. value-driven, fixed costs, variable costs, economies of scale, economies of scope)

business_model_canvas.usage: Brainstorming, pivoting, communicating business model to investors, iterating on startup ideas.

lean_startup_methodology.definition: An approach to building businesses and products developed by Eric Ries, emphasizing rapid experimentation, validated learning, and iterative product development.

build_measure_learn.definition: The fundamental feedback loop: build a minimum viable product, measure customer response, learn whether to pivot or persevere.

minimum_viable_product.definition: The version of a new product that allows a team to collect the maximum amount of validated learning about customers with the least effort.
minimum_viable_product.purpose: Test fundamental business hypotheses before investing significant resources.

validated_learning.definition: Learning backed by empirical data collected from real customers, not opinions or theoretical analysis.

pivot.definition: A structured course correction to test a new fundamental hypothesis about the product, strategy, or engine of growth.

venture_financing.definition: The various methods and stages through which startups and new ventures raise capital.

bootstrapping.definition: Self-funding the venture using personal savings, revenue, or minimal external resources.

angel_investors.definition: High-net-worth individuals who invest their own money in early-stage startups, typically in exchange for equity.

venture_capital.definition: Professional investment firms that manage pooled money from institutional investors and invest in high-growth startups.
key_terms: Carried interest (20% of profits to VC managers)

crowdfunding.regulation: JOBS Act (2012) enabled equity crowdfunding for non-accredited investors via Regulation CF.

accelerators_incubators.accelerators: Fixed-term, cohort-based programs providing mentorship, education, and often seed funding in exchange for equity (e.g., Y Combinator, Techstars).
accelerators_incubators.incubators: Longer-term programs providing resources, office space, and mentorship, typically without taking equity.

market_validation.definition: The process of determining whether a product or service has value to potential customers before building or fully launching it.
steps: Problem identification: Confirm a real, significant problem exists
Customer discovery: Interview potential customers (Steve Blank's method)
Solution validation: Test whether your proposed solution addresses the problem
MVP testing: Build and test a minimum viable product
Market sizing: TAM (Total Addressable Market), SAM (Serviceable Available Market), SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market)

business_plan_fundamentals.definition: A formal written document containing business goals, methods for achieving them, and the timeframe for achievement.

key_sections.executive_summary: 1-2 page overview of the entire plan; written last but placed first. Includes mission, product/service, market opportunity, competitive advantage, financial highlights, and funding request.
key_sections.company_description: Legal structure, history, mission/vision, objectives, and what makes the company unique.
key_sections.organization_and_management: Organizational structure, management team bios, board of advisors, hiring plan.
key_sections.products_or_services: Detailed description, development stage, intellectual property, R&D activities.

components: Marketing mix (4Ps: Product, Price, Place, Promotion)
Funding requirements and use of funds

key_sections.appendix: Supporting documents (resumes, permits, leases, contracts, market research data).

pitch_deck.definition: A brief presentation (10-15 slides) summarizing the business plan for potential investors.
